title: The Rise of Generative UI for Developers (CopilotKit)
summary: CopilotKit is a framework that enables developers to build generative UI applications by connecting AI agents to frontend components. It allows agents to render real UI components, share state with React or Next.js apps, and implement human-in-the-loop approval workflows, addressing the limitation of traditional bolted-on chatbots in SaaS applications.
writeup: Better Stack walks through CopilotKit, a front-end stack for building AI that lives inside a product instead of in a chat box bolted to the side. The live demo shows an agent that renders real UI components, shares live state with a React or Next.js app in both directions, and pauses to ask for approval before it changes anything. The video breaks the framework into four parts (the AG-UI protocol, generative UI, shared-state co-agents, and human-in-the-loop control), weighs it against the lighter Vercel AI SDK, and lands on a clear verdict: worth it for serious in-app agentic UX, overkill for a basic support chatbot.
key_points: CopilotKit is a front-end stack for agentic apps, not just a chat component or a wrapper around an LLM API: it supplies the pieces for AI that acts inside the product.

It rests on four parts: AG-UI, an open event-based protocol between agents and frontends; generative UI, where the agent renders real components instead of only text; shared state (co-agents) that syncs the app and the agent backend in both directions; and human-in-the-loop approval.
The pivotal demo moment is the agent pausing to ask for approval before acting, keeping the user in control where real software demands it.

Versus the Vercel AI SDK, CopilotKit is batteries-included (streaming chat, generative UI, shared state, and approval flows out of the box), while the Vercel AI SDK is lighter and gives more low-level control.

The tradeoffs are real: it is heavier than a minimal SDK, you adopt CopilotKit's patterns, and it is free only to a point, so you have to track what is open source, what needs keys, and what is paid at scale.
Reach for it when an agent should feel native inside a React or Next.js product; skip it for a basic support chatbot or a simple ask-and-answer feature.
